schools.summary: Mark names Christ 'Son of God' (Bede: rising from lesser to greater); Hilary: a true Son by nature, not adoption.
Mark (Peter's interpreter) uniquely begins with the Baptist; the title 'Son of God' echoes Peter's confession (Matthew 16:16).
Calvin: Mark begins with John because the Law's abrogation began with his preaching (Malachi 3:1; John 1:17).
Henry: gospel = God's faithful word; its foundation is that Jesus is the Son of God.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to Matthew 13:12, not this verse.

schools.summary: Mark combines Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3; John is both 'angel' (messenger, by office) and 'voice' preceding the Word (Bede).
Textual debate: 'in the prophets' (Lightfoot, two sources) vs 'in Isaiah' as the harder, better reading (Clarke, Ellicott) — Isaiah as chief of the prophets.

debates.question: Is the better reading 'in the prophets' or 'in Isaiah the prophet'?

schools.summary: The herald's voice (Isaiah 40:3) prepares God's coming; John applied it to himself (John 1:23) — a voice announcing the Word, like a royal courier preparing the king's road.
John preached but could not give remission (Bede, Theophylact); his austere dress and food mark mortification and rebuke.
'For (eis) remission' = 'with reference to'; a baptism on the basis of repentance and confession (Robertson), the sign presupposing penitence and looking to forgiveness (Ellicott).
A steady stream (imperfect tense) from Judea and Jerusalem; public confession of sin, the necessary accompaniment of baptism (cf. proselyte baptism, OT penitence).
Cyril: first the wounds are shown, then the remedy applied — confession before cleansing; put off the old slough of sin.
Rough camel-hair cloth, leathern girdle, locusts and wild honey — the austere life of a wilderness penitent, recalling Elijah (2 Kings 1:8; Malachi 4:5).
Bede/Theophylact: the austere garb signifies mortification (Galatians 5:24) and the grief of repentance he preached.
John's sincere self-effacement; Mark's vivid 'stoop down and unloose the latchet' marks the menial slave's office, magnifying Christ's dignity.
John proclaims Christ first as a man mightier than he, gradually leading hearers to his deity; unworthy even of the servant's station.

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here, on 'Come unto me,' belongs to Matthew 11, not this verse.

schools.summary: John's water-baptism is the symbol; Christ's Spirit-baptism the substance (Acts 2; Joel 2:28); Mark omits 'fire' for Gentile readers (Ellicott).
Jerome: water is man's ministry, the Spirit God's — only God can give the Holy Ghost, a proof of Christ's deity.
The sinless Christ is baptized to fulfil righteousness, sanctify the waters, and be manifested; the Trinity is revealed (Bede) — Son baptized, Spirit descending, Father's voice.
Henry: he took the likeness of sinful flesh, washed as if polluted, sanctifying himself for our sakes (John 17:19).
Mark's 'from Nazareth'; the Baptist saw the Spirit descend (John 1:32); against the Gnostic dove-Christ reading (Robertson).
Mark's bold 'rent open' (vs 'opened') for the heavens; Christ saw them split and the Spirit descend like a dove — the Spirit loving simplicity and abiding on Christ (Bede).
The Father's voice (cf. Psalm 2:7); Mark/Luke have 'Thou art' (to the Son), Matthew 'This is' (to bystanders) — same meaning (Augustine).
Cyril: the Father's witness to the beloved Son refutes the Jews who deny the Only-begotten — 'unless thou be well pleased in Him, thou hast not life.'
Chrysostom: Christ fights Satan after baptism so the baptized may endure temptation; 'driven' shows divine providence — not self-sought, yet conquered.
Mark's bold 'driveth' (the demon-casting word) means impelled/led by the Spirit, not forced against his will (Barnes); the Spirit, not the devil, leads (Bede).
Mark compresses the temptation but adds 'with the wild beasts' — terror and protection, fulfilling Psalm 91:13 (the Psalm Satan misused); the Second Adam where the first fell.
Henry: the Father's care preserved him among the beasts; special protections are earnests of supplies; angels minister after victory (Hebrews 1:14).

outliers.note: The JFB fragment here belongs to Matthew 25, not this verse.

schools.summary: After John's arrest Christ retires to Galilee (teaching prudence in persecution) and preaches the kingdom — the Law complete, the Gospel now at work (Theophylact).
Christ takes up his public ministry, heralding 'the gospel of the kingdom of God' (Mark's phrase for Matthew's 'kingdom of heaven').
Henry: when John was silenced, Christ began to preach — the Word taking up where the voice left off.
'The time is fulfilled' (the fulness of time, Galatians 4:4; Daniel 9); the kingdom is at hand; the two-fold response of repentance and faith ('believe the gospel,' Mark's addition).
A second call (after the meeting in John 1 and the catch of Luke 5); honest fishermen, worthy first disciples; fishers of men make their catch alive, not dead (Remigius).
Mark's realistic glimpse of Christ in action by the sea; the four fishermen-partners (Luke 5:7); unlettered men chosen that faith might rest on God's power, not learning (Bede).
Mark's unique 'make you to become' — Christ undertakes the slow process of forming fishermen into fishers of men; the qualification is his gift, not their attainment.
'Straightway' (Mark's word) they left their nets — perhaps their all — and followed; immediate, whole-hearted obedience the mark of true discipleship.
Wesley: 'Happy they who follow Christ at the first call!' — they forsook their employ and constantly attended him.
Mark's detail: 'a little further' and 'mending their nets' — James and John found at diligent work, called like the first pair from the midst of their daily labour.
Mark's unique 'hired servants' shows the sons of Zebedee were of some means; they left a going business and their father to follow — a real sacrifice, shared by the family (Robertson).
Christ teaches in the Capernaum synagogue (his new headquarters) on the sabbath; Mark's signature 'straightway' (41 times in the Gospel, 9 in ch.1).
He teaches as the Lord of Moses, by his own authority ('But I say unto you'), not as the scribes who only expound (Bede).
The hearers astonished at Christ's authority (Matthew 7:28-29) — power direct from God, unlike the scribes who merely cited traditions (Robertson).
Calvin: the scribes' doctrine was literal and dead, void of the Spirit; Christ's preaching shone with the Spirit's power (1 Corinthians 4:20).
The demon recognizes Christ with knowledge but not charity, fearing judgment (Augustine); 'unclean' marks his impiety; the medicine first works against the author of death (Bede).
Henry: the unclean spirit, having lost all purity, cries out in agony — the devils believe and tremble, with horror but no hope.
The demoniac (with lucid intervals) mingled in the synagogue; Mark distinguishes the man from the unclean spirit in him.
'What have we to do with thee' (Hebrew idiom); 'the Holy One of God' (cf. Psalm 16:10) — a forced confession from demons who knew his power and dreaded destruction.
Knowledge without faith — the demon confesses the One Holy yet without certain knowledge, that the miracle might lead others to believe (Theophylact).

themes: art thou come to destroy us

schools.summary: Christ rebukes the spirit, not the man (proving a real being, not mere disease); 'Hold thy peace' = 'be muzzled/gagged' (the storm-stilling verb, Mark 4:39).
Calvin: Christ refuses the demon's testimony lest his glory be soiled or collusion suspected — we must not believe devils though they speak truth.
The demon convulses the man and screeches but does no lasting harm (Mark's 'torn' reconciled with Luke's 'hurt him not'); a last act of impotent malice (Barnes).
Calvin: the violent departure shows the devil's rage, yet the man is restored to perfect health; deliverance comes with a grievous tumult (Henry).
The miracle confirms the teaching — authority in word (v.22) shown in command over demons; a fresh, original teaching unlike the rabbis' droning (Robertson).
Calvin: they call it 'doctrine' in admiration, not reproach — but err in stopping at astonishment instead of advancing to faith.
Christ's fame spread by word of mouth through all Galilee; the miracle was great, benevolent, and public (Clarke); Mark's frequent 'immediately' drives the pace.
An eyewitness scene (Peter behind Mark) in Peter's own house; the instant, complete cure of his mother-in-law by Christ's touch (1 Corinthians 9:5).
Henry: the cure was complete — she rose strengthened to minister; the healing fits us for service.
Calvin: a private, familiar illustration of grace; a single touch instantly removed a violent disease, occasioning many further miracles.
Gill: the fever was 'great,' violent and life-threatening to an aged woman (Luke); the household's appeal to Christ models the recourse of faith in distress.
Gill: Christ took her hand not to diagnose but to restore; the instant, complete cure (no convalescence) left her strong to minister — healing for service (Henry).
At sunset (sabbath ended) the people bring their sick in a steady stream; Mark's vivid detail, Luke adding Christ touched each one.
The waiting till evening shows sabbath scruple; the setting sun figures Christ's passion and the grace then extended to the Gentiles (Bede).
Mark's vivid detail (from Peter's eyes): the whole city gathered at Peter's door, Christ healing in the sunset glow.
Wesley: 'a fair prospect' — yet these blossoms would die without fruit, for the crowds came for healing, not faith.

themes: all the city at the door

schools.summary: Christ healed all the many brought, of every disease, and silenced the demons who knew him (Luke 4:41) — their testimony would not help his cause (Robertson).
Calvin: two reasons for silence — the time of full revelation not come, and the demons' praise could only soil his honour; Wesley refutes the 'mere disease' theory.
Christ's early, solitary prayer (before dawn); Barnes' meditation — secret morning prayer is vital, else piety withers; the retreat guards him from the crowd's snare.
He prayed not from need but 'by way of an economy,' as our model — attributing all good to God (Theophylact, Pseudo-Chrysostom).
Henry: as man he prayed though as God prayed to; alone with the Father after public labour, a pattern of closet prayer.
The disciples 'hunted him out' (perfective verb) to his place of prayer; a well-meant pursuit (Luke: 'stayed him') that misread his purpose of withdrawal.
Clarke: they followed eagerly, having begun to taste the good word — the fresh zeal of new converts.
The disciples urge Christ back to the seeking crowds; a report mixing pride in his popularity with partiality for their own city (Henry); the seekers' motives were mixed (Clarke).
Christ leaves the Capernaum crowds for the 'village cities' to preach; the preacher must go to the people (Clarke); 'came forth' refers to his mission (Luke: 'sent').
Calvin: an earnest desire to fulfil his office — he must travel throughout Judea to rouse all to hear the Gospel, by the Father's call.

themes: let us go to the next towns

schools.summary: A summary of the Galilean tour — preaching in the synagogues and casting out demons; Clarke: unwearied, persevering diligence, a pattern for ministers.
Henry: 'Christ's doctrine is Satan's destruction' — preaching and exorcism go together, the gospel overthrowing the devil's kingdom.
The leper's faith and submission ('if thou wilt, thou canst'); Christ touches him as Lord of the Law, undefiled, healing by grace above the Law (Bede, Chrysostom).
Henry: how to apply to Christ — humility, firm belief of his power, submission to his will; faith draws out his power into act.
Barnes: the kneeling entreaty, the touch evidencing power already put forth; only God works miracles, yet Christ does it by his own will — therefore Divine.
Mark alone records Christ 'moved with compassion' (splanchnistheis) — not for applause but from genuine pity; the compassion, touch, and sovereign word together cleanse the leper.
Gill: the leprosy departed instantly at Christ's word — no interval, the work being in the command (Bede; Psalm 148:5); leprosy figuring sin, its cleansing the soul's.
Mark's strong word 'straitly charged' (embrimēsamenos, of a horse's snorting) marks Christ's powerful emotion before sin's symbol; the charge to report to the priest and avoid tumult (Robertson).
Gill (with Henry): the stern charge may also warn against the sin that occasioned the leprosy (Miriam, Gehazi, Uzziah) — 'sin no more' (John 5:14).
Calvin: 'a testimony unto them' = to the priests, leaving them inexcusable if they reject Christ, who honoured every point of the Law; the silence prevents slander before the man arrives (Wesley).
The priest's verdict was needed to certify the cure officially (Robertson); Christ avoids tumult and worldly honour (Chrysostom).
The leper's disobedient publicity drove Christ from the cities to desert places, yet crowds sought him from every quarter — well-meant but disregarding his charge (Barnes).
Christ retires to the desert, loving a quiet life; manifested to those who quit the love of praise and follow him into solitude (Bede, Pseudo-Jerome).
